segments.s147-g97: بگفتند is past 3rd plural 'they said', often introducing reported speech or a command. The conjunction و simply links clauses. پس here is an adverb 'then/after that' (or a particle introducing the command). رود is the classical 3rd‑person form of رفتن used to report a directive or event ('let him go' / 'he go/should go'); in context it functions as the content of their saying (they told him to withdraw). می‌خواستند is past imperfect/intentional 'they wanted' or 'they intended' and connects to what follows (it leaves the purpose or result to be completed by the next segment).
segments.s147-g98: همه شب = 'the whole night' (همه as 'whole/entire' + شب 'night'). همی is the archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or habitual aspect ('kept on / continuously'), common in classical Persian. لشکر is the object 'the army/host', and آراستند is past 3rd plural of آراستن 'they arrayed/arranged/embellished' — together: 'they spent the whole night arraying the army.'
segments.s147-g81: بیامد is a simple past third-person singular meaning "he came." پیاده is a manner adjunct placed after the verb meaning "on foot." به نزدیک + اوی is a prepositional phrase: به (towards/to) + نزدیک ("near") + اوی (archaic third-person pronoun referring to "him/his" as the goal), so the phrase means "to his vicinity/near him."
segments.s147-g82: چنین is an adverb meaning "thus/so" modifying the following speech. گفت here functions as the past-tense verb "said/spoke" (classical narrative order: adverb + verb). کای is the vocative particle "O," and مهتر جنگجوی is the addressed title "chief/leader (of) the warrior," with مهتر as the head noun and جنگجوی as a descriptive noun.
segments.s147-g83: This is a question: پیاده is a fronted adverbial/manner word here meaning "on foot." کجا بوده‌ای is the interrogative clause "where have you been?" with بوده‌ای as the second-person perfect/past form "you have been/you were." تیره شب is a temporal adverbial "in the dark night," placed after the clause for emphasis.
segments.s147-g84: تهمتن is the proper/name-epithet (the speaker) in subject position. به گفتار is a prepositional expression meaning "in speech/with words" (به marking manner). بگشاد is an archaic past form meaning "he opened" (the prefix بـ is a classical verbal prefix), and لب is the object "lip(s)," so بگشاد لب = "he opened his lips" i.e. began to speak.
segments.s147-g85: بگفتش is a classical verb form meaning "he told/said to him" with the enclitic -ش marking the indirect object "to him." به گیو repeats the dative as an explicit mention "to Giv." آن کجا کرده بود is an indirect interrogative/nominal clause "where he had been/done" with کرده بود as the past perfect construction "had done/been." The whole segment functions: "He told (it) to Giv — where he had been."
segments.s147-g86: چنان is a determiner/adverb meaning "such" (here modifying the noun), شیرمردی is the noun "brave man/hero." که introduces a relative clause "who/that," and آزرده بود is a past-tense predicate "was distressed/grieved." Thus the segment is a descriptive clause: "such a brave man who was distressed."
segments.s147-g87: وزان is a fused/poetic compound meaning "from that" (و + ز/زان), tying this action to what preceded. جایگه is the place/position from which he departs. رفت is the past verb "he went," and نزدیک شاه is the prepositional phrase "near the king." Word order is typical narrative: (from-that-place) + subject-action + goal.
segments.s147-g88: ز ترکان is a poetic prepositional phrase "about/from the Turks" (ز = "from/of/about"). سخن گفت is the verbal phrase "spoke (words)" or "said" (سخن as object "speech/words" + گفت as verb). وز بزم‌گاه is another fused expression (و + ز) meaning "and from the assembly/banquet-hall," indicating the locus or occasion of the speaking; وز here is the classical linker "and from."
segments.s147-g17: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause; the verb دید (past) follows the object سهراب را, where را is the definite-object marker attached to the name. بر تخت بزم is a locative prepositional phrase ('on the banquet throne') placed after the verb, a typical poetic word order.
segments.s147-g18: نشسته is a participial/adjectival verb meaning 'seated'. به یک دست literally 'in/with one hand' uses به as a prepositional particle; او is the pronoun referring back to the person who saw Sohrab (used here in a possessive/identifying sense), and ژنده‌رزم is a poetic compound describing a ragged war-garment or war-accoutrement (expressed elliptically with the copula omitted).
segments.s147-g19: به دیگر is an adding particle phrase meaning 'another' or 'then another'; چو Hومان is a simile 'like Hūmān'. سوار دلیر is an appositive noun phrase 'a brave rider/horseman' describing that other person. Word order is compact and nominative elements are in apposition.
segments.s147-g20: دگر again marks 'another/one more'; بارمان is the proper name in apposition; نام‌بردار is an epithet meaning 'renowned' or 'famous', and شیر is a metaphorical epithet 'lion' (praising bravery). The sequence is nominative/appositive and the copula is omitted, a common poetic compression.
segments.s147-g21: تو گفتی is the second-person past 'you said' (the reporting clause). همه تخت سهراب بود is a verbless-appearing possession/clause rendered with the copula بود 'was' at the end: 'the whole throne was (belonging to) Sohrab.' The object/topic/order is typical of Persian clauses where the copula appears finally.
segments.s147-g22: بسان introduces a simile 'like/as'. یکی سرو shoud be read 'a single cypress' (یکی = one/a, سرو = cypress), شاداب is the adjective 'fresh/vigorous', and بود is the past copula 'was' concluding the simile-clause.
segments.s147-g23: دو بازو is the nominative 'two arms'. به کردار is a poetic locution 'in the manner/shape of' (کردار used here in a figurative formative sense), and ران هیون is a genitive phrase 'the thigh/haunch of a beast' — together yielding the simile 'arms shaped like a beast's haunches.' Word order puts the simile after the noun it qualifies.
segments.s147-g24: برش here functions as 'his figure/build/profile' (a noun meaning his aspect), چون introduces 'like', بر پیل literally 'on an elephant' = 'like an elephant' (classical usage where بر+animal = comparison to that animal), و links the next simile, and چهره چو خون is 'his face like blood' (i.e., blood-red); the final simile is closed but the copula is omitted in this compressed poetic line.
segments.s147-g49: خروشان is an adverbial/participial adjective here ('roaring') placed before the clause to qualify the action. ازان درد is a prepositional phrase 'from that pain' (ازان = 'from that'), and بازآمدند is the past plural verb 'they returned' (regular past tense, third-person plural) so the clause reads 'they returned from that pain, roaring.'
segments.s147-g50: شگفتی is a noun 'astonishment'; فرو is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'deeply/utterly'; مانده is the past-participial/adjectival form 'remained' or 'was left' (here functioning predicatively). از introduces the cause/target ('at/on account of' or 'because of'), کار is 'deed/act', and ژند names the agent as 'the ragged one' — so the phrase means 'deep astonishment remained because of the deed of the ragged one.'
segments.s147-g51: به سهراب is a dative prepositional phrase 'to Sohrab' (به = 'to/for'); گفتند is the past plural of گفتن ('they said'), here introducing reported speech — note the provided sense list does not include this verbal reading; شد is the past of شدن 'became' used here in the sense 'has become', and ژنده‌رزم is a compound noun 'ragged warrior' functioning as the predicate of that verbal clause.
segments.s147-g52: سرآمد here means 'has ended/the end has come' (noun used as statement of completion). برو is the imperative 'go'. روز پیگار و بزم is a nominal phrase 'the day of battle (پیگار) and feast (بزم)'; the sequence gives the content of the announcement/command: 'the day of battle and feast is at an end — go.'
segments.s147-g53: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when'; بشنید is a past tense verb 'he heard' (perfective third-person); سهراب is the subject; برجست is an intransitive past 'rose up/started up' (a sudden motion); زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the verb sequence.
segments.s147-g54: بیامد is the past 'he came'; بر here marks direction/toward ('to/on/against' → here 'to'); ژنده is the object 'the ragged one'; برسان is a present/immediate infinitival/imperative-like form 'bringing/bringing to' or 'to bring' (in context it functions as a participial notion 'bringing'); دود is the thing brought 'smoke' (likely incense-smoke). The clause thus reads 'he came to the ragged one, bringing smoke.'
segments.s147-g55: A simple coordinate list of nominative nouns joined by و: اباچاکر (a named attendant/title), و شمع ('and candles'), و خنیاگران ('and the minstrels'). The sequence names the people/objects that approach; no verb appears in this segment alone.
segments.s147-g56: بیامد is the past 'came' (third-person); ورا is an enclitic/oblique pronoun meaning 'to him' or 'there' (marks the patient/target); دید is the past 'saw' (they beheld); مرده is the predicative/adjectival past participle 'dead'; چنان is an adverb 'thus/like that' — together: 'they came and saw him dead like that.'
segments.s147-g25: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/among' marking the group to be searched. ترکان is the noun 'the Turks.' بگرد is an imperative 'look/search!' addressed to someone (literary command). اندِرش is a contraction of اندر + ش (اندر = 'in/among'; ش = enclitic pronoun), so 'in them' or 'among them.' دلیر is an adjective 'brave' that will modify the following noun phrase.
segments.s147-g26: جوان is used as a substantive 'young men' (here plural sense). و simply links adjectives/phrases. سرافراز is an epithet meaning 'proud/exalted.' چون is the comparative particle 'like/as.' نره‌شیر is a simile head 'male-lion' — the whole clause says these young men are 'proud like a male lion.'
segments.s147-g27: پرستار functions as an agent/attendant (an archaic or poetic word for one who serves). پنجاه gives the number 'fifty.' با + دست‌بند means 'with handcuffs/bracelets'; given the context, دست‌بند here is best read as manacles or captive bracelets rather than merely decorative jewelry.
segments.s147-g28: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before.' دل‌افروز is an attributive adjective 'delightful/heart-pleasing' describing the تخت. تخت بلند is a nominal phrase 'the high/tall throne' (بلند = 'high/lofty') — overall a locative phrase placing the attendants in front of the splendid throne.
segments.s147-g29: همی is a frequentative/intensifying particle used in epic narration ('continually' / 'again and again'). یک به یک means 'one by one.' خواندند is the past plural 'they called/cried out' (here used with آفرین). آفرین is the acclamation 'Bravo!' or 'Well done!' so the clause means 'they one by one shouted “Bravo!”.'
segments.s147-g30: بران is a deictic/relational particle here meaning 'upon him' / 'for him' (marking the target of the praise). برز و بالا are paired epithets meaning 'exalted / lofty' (nominalized qualities), and تیغ و نگین enumerate his martial and ornamental attributes ('sword and gem'). The list is the content of their acclamation — praise for his rank, sword and jewel.
segments.s147-g31: همی again signals the narrative continuity ('then/so'). دید is the simple past 'saw' with رستم as subject. مر او را is an older/clipped pronominal sequence: مر is an archaic enclitic pronoun and او را is the explicit object marking 'him' — together 'he saw him.' ز دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar / at a distance.'
segments.s147-g32: نشست is the past 'he sat (down)' or 'took a seat.' و links the two actions. نگه کرد is a compound where نگه ('gaze/watch') plus کرد ('did/made') forms 'kept watch' or 'looked intently.' مردان سور is the object phrase 'the men of the feast' (سور = banquet/feast), so the whole clause says he sat and watched the men at the feast.
segments.s147-g9: بدو گفت کاووس is inverted word order common in poetry: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + کاووس (Kavus) = "Kavus said to him." کین کار تست is a noun phrase where کین (vengeance) modifies کار (deed/affair). تست here is an archaic/poetic pronominal/clitic meaning roughly "(it) is thine/your" (compare modern توست), so the phrase = "this is your deed of vengeance."
segments.s147-g10: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause attached to the preceding phrase ("which/is that"). The adjectives follow without a copula: بیداردل is a compound adjective 'wakeful‑hearted' (vigilant / watchful in heart), بادی is a poetic simile/adjective literally 'wind‑borne' (i.e. swift or like the wind), و is the conjunction 'and', and تن‌درست is an adjective 'sound/healthy in body.'
segments.s147-g11: This is a nominal phrase: تهمتن is the hero's epithet/name, followed by یکی (a certain / one) as an indefinite determiner and جامه (garment). ترکوار is a postpositive adjective 'Turk‑like' modifying جامه — Persian adjectives often follow the noun they qualify.
segments.s147-g12: بپوشید is the past tense 'he put on' (perfective). و آمد دوان links two actions: و آمد (and he came) with دوان functioning adverbially/participially 'running' or 'in haste' describing the manner. تا حصار uses تا + noun to mark the endpoint 'as far as / up to the fortress.'
segments.s147-g13: بیامد is a simple past 'he came.' چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning 'when.' نزدیکی دژ is a genitive/possessive construction 'the nearness/proximity of the fortress' (word order noun + modifier), and رسید is 'he reached/arrived (at).' Together: 'when he came and drew near the fortress.'
segments.s147-g14: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/masdar 'roaring' or 'outcry.' نوش here is an interjection/onomatopoetic particle representing the shout/cheer (poetic exclamation). ترکان is the genitive/agent 'of the Turks,' and شنید is the past 'he heard.' Word order places the action-noun before شنید (object before verb) as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s147-g15: بران is a locative/demonstrative adverb 'upon that / into that' pointing back to the previously mentioned place. دژ درون رفت literally 'entered into the fortress' (دژ = fortress, درون = inside, رفت = went/entered). مرد دلیر is an appositive/subject phrase 'the brave man' (دلیر following the noun as an adjective).
segments.s147-g16: چنان چون is a doubled comparative/simile ('just as' / 'so... as'), introducing the likeness: سوی آهوان means 'toward the gazelles' (سوی = toward(s); آهوان = deer/gazelles). نره‌شیر is a simile/appositive 'male‑lion' used of the man — here figuratively ('lion‑like man'). The verb of motion is understood from the previous clause, so this is a vivid descriptive simile attached to 'مرد دلیر.'
segments.s147-g73: به ره بر گو پیلتن را بدید is a compact past-tense clause: به ره بر (on the road/along the way) is an adverbial phrase; پیلتن را is the definite object (را marks it), and بدید is the past verb 'he saw'. The subject is omitted (implicit 'he'). Word order is adverbial phrase → (prepositional) marker → object → verb, typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s147-g74: بزد دست و گرز از میان برکشید contains two coordinated actions (بزد … و … برکشید) with an understood single subject. بزد دست literally 'he struck (with) his hand' (بزد = past ‘he struck’). از میان + برکشید means 'drew/raised (it) out from among (them)' — here گرز is the object (mace) and برکشید is the past verb 'he drew/raised'.
segments.s147-g75: یکی بر خروشید چون پیل مست: یکی ('one') is an indefinite subject 'one (of them)'; بر is a verbal prefix here (برخروشیدن = burst into roar) and خروشید is the past 'roared'. چون + noun is a simile: چون پیل مست = 'like a drunken elephant.' The whole is a single past event description.
segments.s147-g76: سپر بر سر آورد و بنمود دست has two coordinated past actions by the same subject: سپر بر سر آورد 'he put/raised the shield upon his head' (آورد = brought/placed) and و بنمود دست 'and he displayed/revealed his hand' (بنمود = showed). Note the object دست follows the verb بنمود in this poetic order.
segments.s147-g77: بدانست رستم کز ایران سپاه: بدانست is a past-tense reporting verb 'he knew/realized'; the following clause is introduced by کز (a fused form meaning 'that/from which'), so رستم is the agent of knowing and کز ایران سپاه begins the content of what he knew ('that an army/from Iran…'). Word order places the reporting verb before the subordinate clause.
segments.s147-g78: به شب گیو باشد طلایه به راه: this is the subordinate clause giving the content of his knowledge. به شب 'by/at night' is an adverbial time phrase; گیو is the proper name (the expected vanguard commander); باشد is a present/subjunctive-form of 'to be' used narratively ('would be' / 'is to be'); طلایه به راه 'the vanguard on the road' is the predicate. The order puts time → subject → auxiliary → predicate.
segments.s147-g79: بخندید و زان پس فغان برکشید links two past actions: بخندید 'he/they laughed' followed by زان پس ('after that') and فغان برکشید 'raised a cry' (فغان as object; برکشید here used metaphorically 'to lift/raise' a cry). The conjunction و ties the actions; زان پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that/then'.
segments.s147-g80: طلایه چو آواز رستم شنید is a temporal subordinate clause: طلایه 'the vanguard' is the subject, چو 'when' introduces the clause, آواز رستم 'Rostam's voice/call' is the object, and شنید is the past verb 'heard'. The clause attaches to the previous action to show when they raised the cry.
segments.s147-g1: چو is an archaic/subordinate conjunction here meaning 'when' (introduces a temporal clause). خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. گشت is the past form of گشتن meaning 'became/turned' or here 'was/was made' (i.e. 'disappeared' with the following phrase). از جهان ناپدید is a prepositional phrase 'vanished from the world' (از + place + adjective), with ناپدید functioning as predicate adjective 'vanished'.
segments.s147-g2: شب تیره is a nominal subject phrase 'the dark night' (adjective after noun is permitted in poetry). بر دشت is a locative prepositional phrase 'upon/over the plain'. لشکر کشید is the main verb phrase: کشید is the past of کشیدن 'to draw/bring/dispatch' and here is used poetically with لشکر as its object ('mustering/drawing a host'); the sentence personifies the night as sending forth a host.
segments.s147-g3: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet used here (address/name). بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' (به + نزدیک + noun), so literal order 'came to near the king' = 'came near the king'.
segments.s147-g4: This is a compact appositive/participial description of Tahmatan: میان بسته is a participial phrase 'having bound/with ... bound in the midst' (میان = in the midst/middle; بسته = bound/tied). جنگ و دل lists two nouns 'war and heart' that are bound together/metaphorically engaged, and کینه‌خواه is an epithet in apposition meaning 'vengeful' or 'seeker of vengeance'. The clause lacks an explicit copula because it's descriptive/appositive.
segments.s147-g5: که introduces a subordinate clause expressing a wish/requirement ('that' / 'so that'). دستور باشد uses باشد (subjunctive/optative form of بودن) to mean 'may there be / let there be an order'. مرا is the object 'me' (here written as م + را in the segmentation: م = me, را = definite/object marker). تاجور is either a vocative 'O merchant' or the addressee/agent; the exact syntactic role is ambiguous in poetry but it names the person involved.
segments.s147-g6: از ایدر is a prepositional phrase 'from/hence, from here' (ایدِر is an archaic/poetic variant of 'here'). شوم is the first‑person subjunctive/potential 'I may become' (expressing the hoped-for outcome). بی‌کلاه و کمر are coordinated adjectives/nominal phrases 'hatless and beltless' (کمر here means 'girdle/belt'), describing the speaker's resulting state.
segments.s147-g7: ببینم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form 'let me see / I will see' expressing intent or immediate action. که introduces an indirect question 'who/which'. این is the demonstrative 'this'. نوجهاندار is a compound noun 'mourner' or 'lamenter' used as the object of the question. کیست is formed from کی 'who' + ست (copula 'is'), giving 'who is ...?'.
segments.s147-g8: بزرگان is the plural noun 'the great ones / elders'. کدامند is an interrogative plural verb 'which/which of them are they?' The conjunction و links the next short question. سالار کیست is another interrogative 'who is the commander/leader?' (کی = who, ست = is). The sequence lists questions the speaker wants answered.
segments.s147-g57: شگفت آمدش is an impersonal, poetic way to say “a wonder came to him” = “he was amazed”; آمدش contains the clitic -ش “to him.” سخت here functions as an adverbial intensifier (“very/strongly”). خیره بماند is a compound: خیره (astonished/staring) + بماند (remained/was left), giving “remained stunned.” The verb forms are narrative/past in tone (poetic register).
segments.s147-g58: دلیران و گردن‌کشان را places two definite groups as the object (را marks the definite object) and بخواند is the past-tense, narrative use of خواندن here meaning “call/summon.” Word order (object(s) before verb) and را emphasize that he summoned those people.
segments.s147-g59: چنین گفت is the formulaic “thus he said” (چنین = thus). گفت is the speech verb (past narrative) introducing reported speech. کامشب = “this/that night” (poetic compound). نباید + verb expresses impersonal prohibition; here نباید غنود means “one must not sleep” (غنود = fall asleep/slumber, in subjunctive/infinitive-like form after نباید).
segments.s147-g60: همه‌شب همی is an adverbial phrase “all night long / indeed all night.” The clause نیزه باید بسود uses باید (necessitative/modal “must/ought”) followed by بسود, here functioning as the verb meaning roughly “be of use / avail.” The literal order is ‘spear must benefit’ = ‘the spear must be useful/ready the whole night.’
segments.s147-g61: که here introduces an explanatory/circumstantial clause (“for/when”). گرگ اندر آمد is an archaic phrasing: اندر آمد = “came in / entered.” میان رمه = “into/among the flock” (میان = among/between, رمه = flock). The clause gives the reason/circumstance for the preceding injunction.
segments.s147-g62: سگ و مرد را آزمودش همه: آزمودش is the past transitive ‘tested’ with the enclitic -ش indicating the thing/agent involved (poetic object/old-style clitic); را again marks the definite object(s). همه at the end emphasizes that it tested them all. In sense: the wolf tested both dogs and men (their courage/effectiveness).
segments.s147-g63: اگر introduces a conditional (“if”). یار here means ally/companion (“be a friend/ally”), and باشد is the present/subjunctive form of بودن used in a conditional clause (“be”). جهان‌آفرین is a title for God or the Creator; the phrase literally says “if the Creator is (my) ally.”
segments.s147-g64: چو = “when.” نعل سمندم is a genitive-chain: نعل (horseshoe) + سمندم (my steed; -م = my). بساید زمین is a poetic verb phrase meaning roughly “strikes/touches the earth” in this context; the verb form is archaic/poetic — the provided sense list does not include the exact ‘strike/touch’ meaning, so the reading is contextual: 'when my horse's shoe hits the ground.' زمین = the ground/earth.
segments.s147-g89: The preposition ز (z) starts a series of genitive/partitive phrases ('from Sohrab, from ...'). The sequence uses poetic repetition (ز ... و از ...) to list aspects of Sohrab. بالای is a noun 'bālā' 'height/upper part' with the enclitic اوی = 'his' attached (old Persian possessive), so بالای اوی = 'his height/loftiness.' Word order is noun + possessive enclitic, typical in Ferdowsi.
segments.s147-g90: Again introduced by the preposition ز 'from', followed by a pair of body parts linked by و. بازوی is the singular noun 'arm'; کتف is 'shoulder'. دلارای is an attributive adjective 'charming/beautiful' modifying کتف, and اوی is the possessive enclitic 'his' attached after it, so کتف دلارای اوی = 'his graceful shoulder.'
segments.s147-g91: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'). هرگز + نخاست forms the negative past 'never did ... arise/appear.' ز ترکان is an ablative/partitive phrase 'from/among the Turks.' چنین کس = 'such a person.' Note نخاست here means 'did not arise/appear' (archaic past).
segments.s147-g92: بکردار is an adjectival/epithetic phrase meaning 'of (good) bearing/form' or 'virtuous/in fine form.' سروست (sorvast) likens the person to a cypress (سرو) with the copular -ست (= 'is'), a common Persian simile for tall/slender bearing. بالاش = بالا + ش (his) 'his upper part/height,' and راست = 'straight'; here راست describes بالاش: 'his upper part stood straight.'
segments.s147-g93: به توران و ایران 'in/for Turan and Iran' is the locative/topic phrase. نماند is past 'remained/was left' used here in the negative sense ('there remained no one'), and به کس 'anyone' completes the negative: 'no one remained.' Word order places the place phrase first, then the verb phrase, typical in epic narrative.
segments.s147-g94: تو گویی is a formulaic literary phrase meaning 'one might say' or 'it seems as if'; here it introduces a figurative remark. که introduces the clause 'that Sam is mounted.' سام is the subject; سوارست is a compound adjective/verb 'is mounted/armoured' (present-state description). و بس = 'and enough' / 'and that's all' (closing particle). Note: the provided lemma senses for گویی do not include the verbal/phrasing sense, so its sense is marked -1.
segments.s147-g95: وزان = و +زان 'and from that' or 'from that (blow)' — a connective that points back to an action just described. مشت 'a blow/fist' is the direct object, with the prepositional phrase بر گردن 'upon the neck' marking the target; ژنده‌رزم is a descriptive compound 'ragged warrior' serving as the possessor/target of گردن.
segments.s147-g96: کزان 'from which' (relative/ablative) + پس 'after that' gives a temporal sequence 'after that.' نیامد is the simple past negative 'did not come/return.' The parallel phrases به رزم و به بزم 'to battle and to the feast' are coordinated objects introduced by repeated به, a typical rhetorical device in classical Persian.
segments.s147-g65: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before two nouns in a tight sequence (فتراک زین) meaning 'from the saddle-cloth and (from) the saddle'. برگشایم is first-person singular present/subjunctive (or optative) of برگشادن, 'I open/unloose/unfurl', and کمند is the direct object 'snare/lasso' that برگشایم governs. Word order is poetic: preposition + two nouns + verb + object, where برگشایم is the speaker's intention to perform the action.
segments.s147-g66: بخواهم is first-person present/subjunctive of خواستن, here 'I wish / I will demand'; از introduces the agent/source 'from the Iranians' (ایرانیان). کین ژند is a terse, poetic noun phrase: کین 'vengeance/grudge' followed by ژند (a rare word meaning 'rag' or 'ragged person'); the phrase is elliptical and functions as the object of بخواهم (what he seeks from the Iranians).
segments.s147-g67: بیامد نشست is a two-part verbal sequence 'he came and sat' (بیامد = came, نشست = sat/settled). از بر گاه خویش is a prepositional/nominal phrase: از بر (classical combination) + گاه 'place/time/seat' + خویش 'his' (here expressed as separate ش), together meaning 'at/from his own seat/court'. The clause states the subject's movement and sitting at his proper place.
segments.s147-g68: گرانمایگان ('the illustrious/esteemed ones') is the definite group; را is the Persian definite/direct-object marker attached to the group; همه 'all/everyone' emphasizes totality. خواند here is used in the sense 'to call/summon' (not 'to read'), and پیش 'before/in the presence of' marks the direction/place of the summons. Word order: object + را + adjunct + verb + prepositional place.
segments.s147-g69: که‌گر is the contracted classical conjunction 'که اگر' meaning 'if'. کم شد 'became few / was lacking' (شد as past of شدن) governs the following prepositional phrase از تخت من 'from my throne' (تخت = throne). ژنده‌رزم ('ragged-warrior') is the noun indicating who would be lacking; the whole clause expresses a conditional 'if it happens that a ragged warrior is missing from my throne'.
segments.s147-g70: نیامد here is negative past 'did not come' (used in a counterfactual/conditional sense 'would not come'); همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'ever/at all' or continuous aspectually. سیر is a nominal meaning 'satisfaction/satiety' (here 'satisfied'), and جانم 'my life/my soul' uses the enclitic possessive -م; the clause says that, in that case, 'my soul would not leave the feast satisfied'—ز + بزم marks origin/place 'from the banquet/assembly'.
segments.s147-g71: چو is the classical subordinating conjunction 'when'. برگشت is the verbal noun/past concept 'returned'; the phrase 'چو برگشت رستم بر شهریار' literally places Rostam's return in relation to the king ('to/into the king's presence'): بر here has the sense 'to/toward' and شهریار is the addressee/recipient 'the king'.
segments.s147-g72: This is a compressed predicative phrase: از ایران سپه 'of/from Iran (the) army' identifies the force; گیو is the proper name 'Giv'; پاسدار is the predicate noun 'guardian/keeper'. The middle item بد is problematic in the printed senses (normally 'bad'); here it functions historically as a contracted/copular/auxiliary residue (equivalent to 'was' / 'became' in classical usage) or as a tersely placed copula, so the whole means 'Giv (was/made) guardian of Iran's army.'
segments.s147-g41: بدان جایگه is an old-fashioned contraction of به + آن جایگه = 'to/at that place' (word-order: prepositional phrase precedes the verb). خشک شد is a past-tense compound (خشک + شد) meaning 'became dry / fell still' — شد is the past of شدن 'to become'. ژنده‌رزم is the noun (epithet) 'the Ragged Warrior' and functions as the subject that underwent شدن.
segments.s147-g42: نشد is the negated past of شدن here ('did not happen / did not become'), with the following noun ژنده‌رزم repeated for emphasis as topic/subject. آنگهی 'then' (temporal) links the negative result to what follows, and سوی بزم is a directional phrase 'toward the banquet' (سوی + بزم). The clause therefore says the Ragged Warrior did not (go/appear) then toward the banquet.
segments.s147-g43: زمانی همی بود is a temporal phrase: زمانی 'for a while' + همی (classical particle often marking continued action) + بود (past of بودن) — together 'he remained/waited for a while.' سهراب is the subject placed after the verb for emphasis (in verse the name can follow the predicate). دیر at the end qualifies the lateness/length of delay.
segments.s147-g44: نیامد is the simple past negative ('did not come' here). به نزدیک او is a prepositional phrase 'to/near him' (به + نزدیک + object pronoun او). ژند شیر is a compound name/epithet (ژند 'the ragged one' + شیر 'lion') functioning as the expected arrival who in fact did not come.
segments.s147-g45: بپرسید is a past tense verb 'asked' (object omitted here but supplied by the following clause). سهراب names the asker (postverbal for emphasis). تا here introduces the subordinate clause that reports what he inquired about (classical/historic usage introducing indirect question) and is followed by the topic noun ژنده‌رزم 'the Ragged Warrior'.
segments.s147-g46: کجا شد is the interrogative 'where did [he] go / where has [he] become' (شد as past of شدن used idiomatically for 'go / be found'). که here introduces a result/explanatory clause 'that...' (i.e. 'that his place is empty'), جایش combines جای + ش (his place), تهی شد is 'became empty' (تهی + شد), and ز بزم is a poetic 'from the banquet' explaining the emptiness of his seat.
segments.s147-g47: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/departed.' و simply links clauses. دیدنش is the suffixed infinitive/masdar form of دیدن + -ش (seeing him / to see him) functioning here as a direct object of the action 'they went and saw him.' افگنده is a past-participial adjective 'cast down' and خوار is an adjective 'humiliated/despised' describing his condition.
segments.s147-g48: برآسوده is a compound adjective 'at ease / relieved' (here ironic/passive sense 'freed'). از بزم و از کارزار is a parallel prepositional phrase 'from the banquet and from the battlefield' explaining what he is now relieved/finished with; از is the poetic 'from' and کارزار is 'battle/struggle.' The entire phrase modifies the prior description of his state.
segments.s147-g33: This phrase = 'به + شایسته‌کاری + برون‌رفت' means 'by/through a meritorious deed (he) found a way out'. 'به' is the preposition 'by/through' here; 'شایسته‌کاری' is a noun 'meritorious act'; 'برون‌رفت' is the nominalized idea 'a way out' (the verbal sense 'finding a way out'). The subject 'ژند' (the ragged man) follows by inversion, a common poetic word order.
segments.s147-g34: 'گوی' here is a nominal/participial use related to گفتن (literally 'saying' or 'he cried'); 'دید' is the simple past 'saw' (here 'having seen'); 'برسان' is an imperative or hortative 'bring/let bring/announce'; 'سرو بلند' is the noun phrase 'tall cypress'. The sequence says that, on seeing the tall cypress, he (the ragged man) cried a command to bring or present it.
segments.s147-g35: 'بدان' is an archaic/contractional locative 'به + آن' meaning 'in/among that'; 'لشکر' = 'the host/army'; 'اندر' = 'in/within' (intensifying location); 'چنو' = 'such/as (like)'; 'کس نبود' = 'there was no one' (کس with the past copula نبود gives the negative existential). Together: 'In that army there was no one like (him).' Note: here the parser split 'بدان' into two tokens; treat them as the fused locative.
segments.s147-g36: 'بر' = 'toward/to' (he moved toward someone); 'رستم' is the proper name; 'آمد' is the past 'came'; 'بپرسید' here functions as a past narrative 'he asked' (epic/older forms often show a prefixal present-looking form for past meaning); 'زود' is an adverb 'quickly/soon'. So: 'he came to Rostam and asked quickly.'
segments.s147-g37: 'چه مردی؟' is a direct question/exclamation 'what sort of man are you?' or 'who are you?'. 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). 'با من بگوی' is an imperative/jussive construction 'speak with me' or 'tell me (of yourself)'; here بگوی is a hortative/imperative form of گفتن addressed to the stranger.
segments.s147-g38: 'سوی' = 'toward'; 'روشنی' = 'the light' (i.e. into the open/lamplight); 'آی' is the imperative 'come!'; 'و' = 'and'; 'بنمای' is an imperative 'show!' (archaic form, literally 'do-show'); 'روی' = 'face'. The speaker orders the person to come into the light and show his face.
segments.s147-g39: 'تهمتن' is an epithet/name (Tahmētan, here the hero). 'یکی مشت' = 'one blow' or 'a single fist/blow' (یکی = one/a certain). 'بر گردنش' = 'upon his neck' (بر = on/upon; گردن = neck; the final 'ش' is the possessive suffix 'his'). The phrase describes the single decisive blow to the neck.
segments.s147-g40: 'بزد' is the past 'he struck'. 'تیز' here functions adverbially 'sharply/forcefully' (poetic use). 'و' = 'and'. 'برشد' is an archaic/perfective form 'it rose' (here of the soul). 'روان' = 'soul/spirit'; 'از تنش' = 'from his body' (از = from; تن + ش = his body). The clause reports that the soul left the body after the blow.

sentences.s147-s49: They told him to go back and meant to; all night long they kept arraying the army.
sentences.s147-s41: He came on foot up to his side; thus he said, "O chief warrior,"
sentences.s147-s42: “On foot — where have you been in the dark night?” Tahmtn opened his lips to speak.
sentences.s147-s43: He told Giv where he had been; such was the brave man who had been distressed.
sentences.s147-s44: From that place he went near the king; he spoke of the Turks and from the assembly-hall.
sentences.s147-s9: When he saw Sohrab seated on the banquet throne, he had in one hand a tattered war-cloak.
sentences.s147-s10: Then another, like Hūmān, a brave horseman; another, Barmān, famed — a lion.
sentences.s147-s11: You said the whole throne was Sohrab’s; it was like a single, fresh cypress.
sentences.s147-s12: Two arms, in form like the haunches of a beast; his build like an elephant’s, and his face like blood.
sentences.s147-s25: They returned from that pain, roaring; deep astonishment remained at the deed of the ragged one.
sentences.s147-s26: They said to Sohrab, 'He has become a ragged warrior; the day of battle and feast is over — go.'
sentences.s147-s27: When Sohrab heard, he rose up quickly and came to the ragged man, bringing smoke.
sentences.s147-s28: Abachakr, the candles, and the minstrels came, and they saw him dead like that.
sentences.s147-s13: Search among the Turks — within them there are a hundred brave young men, proud as a male lion.
sentences.s147-s14: An attendant — fifty of them with bracelets (handcuffs) — stood before the delightful high throne.
sentences.s147-s15: One by one they cried 'Bravo!' — praising him for his greatness and high rank, and for his sword and gem.
sentences.s147-s16: Rostam saw him from afar; he sat down and watched the men at the feast.
sentences.s147-s5: Kavus said to him: this is a deed of vengeance of yours — you are wakeful of heart, like the wind, and sound in body.
sentences.s147-s6: Tahmatan put on a Turk‑like garment and ran to the fortress.
sentences.s147-s7: He came; when he drew near the fortress he heard the Turks' roar.
sentences.s147-s8: He went into that fortress; the brave man, like a male‑lion, charged toward the gazelles.
sentences.s147-s37: On the road he saw Go-Piltan; he struck out with his hand and drew his mace from among them.
sentences.s147-s38: One of them roared like a drunken elephant; he raised his shield over his head and showed his hand.
sentences.s147-s39: Rostam realized that from the Iranian army, by night, Giv would be the vanguard on the road.
sentences.s147-s40: He laughed, and then when the vanguard heard Rostam's voice they raised a cry.
sentences.s147-s1: When the sun disappeared from the world, a dark night drew its host across the plain.
sentences.s147-s2: Tahmatan came up close to the king; in him war and heart were bound — vengeful.
sentences.s147-s3: That there be an order for me, O merchant — that hence I become hatless and beltless.
sentences.s147-s4: Let me see who this mourner is; who the great ones are, and who is the commander.
sentences.s147-s29: He was greatly astonished and stood dumbfounded; he summoned the brave and the rebellious.
sentences.s147-s30: Thus he said, 'One must not sleep tonight; the spear must be of use all night.'
sentences.s147-s31: For when a wolf came into the midst of the flock, it put both dog and man to the test.
sentences.s147-s32: If the Creator be a friend, when my steed’s horseshoe strikes the ground…
sentences.s147-s45: From Sohrab — from his stature and lofty height, from his arm and his graceful shoulder.
sentences.s147-s46: For never among the Turks did such a man arise; he was cypress‑formed in bearing and his upper part stood straight.
sentences.s147-s47: There remained no one in Turan or Iran; you would say that Sam alone was mounted.
sentences.s147-s48: From that blow on the ragged warrior's neck — after that he did not return to battle or to the feast.
sentences.s147-s33: I will unfurl a snare from the saddle-cloth and the saddle; I will demand from the Iranians the vengeance-rag.
sentences.s147-s34: He came and sat at his court; he summoned all the illustrious ones before him.
sentences.s147-s35: For if a ragged warrior were lacking at my throne, my life would not come away satisfied from the feast.
sentences.s147-s36: When Rostam returned to the king, Giv was (made) the guardian of Iran's army.
sentences.s147-s21: At that spot the Ragged Warrior fell still; he did not then go toward the banquet.
sentences.s147-s22: For a while Sohrab remained; Zhend-Shir did not come near him.
sentences.s147-s23: Sohrab asked, 'Where has the Ragged Warrior gone, that his place at the banquet is empty?'
sentences.s147-s24: They went and found him prostrate and humbled, freed from the banquet and from the battlefield.
sentences.s147-s17: By a meritorious deed he made his way out; the ragged man, seeing a tall cypress, cried, "Bring it!"
sentences.s147-s18: In that host there was no one like him; he came up to Rostam and asked quickly.
sentences.s147-s19: "What man are you?" he said to him. "Speak with me; come into the light and show your face."
sentences.s147-s20: Tahmētan delivered a single blow upon his neck; he struck sharply, and his soul rose from his body.
